_doc: Phase X Step 2 — Editorial lens registry. Each lens is a depth-of-inquiry frame that the Original Thinking generator uses to produce non-derivative topics. The registry is hand-curated; do NOT generate lenses programmatically. Add new lenses sparingly — every lens shapes the editorial voice of the show.

lenses.description: The slow erosion that happens when someone trades a piece of who they are for safety, money, status, or belonging — and the moment they recognize the trade.
question_kinds: When did you first know you were performing instead of being?
What part of you did you mute to be accepted?
What would the version of you at 17 say to you now?
avoid: Career-pivot stories without the inner cost

lenses.description: Loss that doesn't fit a recognized name — the death of a future, of a friendship, of an identity, of a homeland in memory. Grief no one allows you to claim.
question_kinds: What loss did you have to carry alone because no one knew it was a loss?
What did you grieve quietly while the world expected you to be fine?
Whom did you lose without their dying?

lenses.description: The small, defensible compromise that compounds — the lines you didn't notice you crossed because each step was 'reasonable.' The biography of a slow ethical drift.
question_kinds: What's the smallest decision you regret most?
When did you realize you were no longer the person who would have said no?
What did you tell yourself to make it okay?

lenses.description: The quiet 4 a.m. awareness that nothing means what we pretend it means — and the question of how a person keeps moving anyway. Not nihilism; the courage to live without certainty.
question_kinds: What do you do with the knowledge that none of this is permanent?
When do you feel most awake to the meaninglessness — and how do you get up?
What truth would break you if you let it in fully?

lenses.description: The way unequal power — money, fame, gender, age, employer/employee — distorts what we call love, friendship, mentorship. The honest conversation no one wants to have about closeness.
question_kinds: When did you mistake admiration for love?
What does a person owe someone who needed them?
Who in your life had power over you that you didn't see at the time?

lenses.description: The patterns set before language — the silences, the looks, the small humiliations — that we keep replaying as adults without ever naming them. The ghost dictating today's choices.
question_kinds: What did you learn about love before you were old enough to question it?
Whose voice do you still hear in your worst self-talk?
What scene from childhood still runs in the back of your mind?

lenses.description: The specific isolation of arriving — when the people who were with you on the way up don't fit the room you're in, and the people in the room don't know who you used to be.
question_kinds: Who can you no longer call?
What did you have to leave behind to be where you are?
When do you feel most alone now that you've 'made it'?

lenses.description: The thing you have never told anyone — not because it's evil, but because telling it would change how you're seen. The secret that defines a life from underneath.
question_kinds: What story have you told a thousand times leaving out the one part that matters?
What would you not survive being known for?
What part of yourself do you protect by never naming?

lenses.description: Loving someone whose mind, body, or future cannot guarantee they will stay — illness, distance, instability — and the discipline of staying anyway. Love without the insurance policy.
question_kinds: What did you commit to knowing it might not last?
How do you love someone you might lose tomorrow?
When did love stop feeling safe and start feeling true?

lenses.description: The paradox of wanting to be known and dreading it equally — the masks people build out of competence, charm, or silence to be loved at a distance.
question_kinds: What part of you flinches when someone gets too close?
What would change if your most trusted friend saw the unedited you?
What do you protect by being impressive?

lenses.description: The lived experience of being multiple selves at once — across languages, generations, classes, geographies — and the cost of having no single room where every fragment is welcome.
question_kinds: Where do you feel most yourself, and where do you feel most a stranger?
Which version of you has paid the highest price?
What were you taught to hide to belong?

lenses.description: The things families do not say — the war, the disappearance, the addiction, the love that wasn't allowed — and the way that silence is passed down through generations as a shape, not a story.
question_kinds: What did your family agree, without speaking, never to discuss?
What did your parents protect you from by not telling you?
What story in your family was always told around something missing?
